content: When you first start exporting Rust functions to JavaScript using wasm-bindgen, the most common pitfall is treating JsValue as a universal catch-all instead of recognizing it as an opaque type with specific behavior. You cannot assume that every value exposed to JS will be accessible from Rust; in fact, many operations are restricted by design. For instance, if you try to dereference a JsValue to get the inner type without knowing what was passed in, you risk panicking at runtime because the type system does not enforce safety on the host side. The correct approach is to treat JsValue as a handle that requires explicit conversion using methods like from_js_value or into_raw_val only when you have validated the input. This distinction becomes critical when combining wasm-bindgen with other systems like WebAssembly modules, where memory management and type coercion must be handled carefully. If you are building a bridge between Rust and JavaScript, remember that JsValue is not just any value; it is specifically designed to carry values across the boundary while preserving type information only when explicitly requested. You should never blindly dereference a JsValue assuming it holds a known type like String or Number without first checking its kind using methods such as is_string or is_number. Furthermore, consider how wasm-pack build handles these exports during compilation; it automatically generates glue code that wraps your Rust functions but does not guarantee full interoperability for every possible JS construct. For example, if you pass a complex object to a function expecting JsValue, the compiler may fail silently or produce unexpected results depending on whether the binding was explicitly annotated with #[wasm_bindgen]. The key insight is that wasm-bindgen attributes provide context-aware type conversions but do not replace your responsibility for validating input types at runtime. When exporting functions, you must decide between using explicit type annotations like #[wasm_bindgen] or relying on default JsValue handling, and this choice depends heavily on whether you need strict typing or maximum flexibility. If you choose the latter, anticipate that performance might suffer slightly due to additional checks performed by wasm-bindgen during runtime execution. Another subtle interaction involves how JsValue interacts with async operations; when exposing asynchronous Rust code to JS, the generated glue code must manage the lifetime of promises and handle potential cancellation scenarios gracefully. You will often encounter edge cases where a simple function call fails because the JavaScript side did not properly escape quotes or handle null values correctly. In these situations, your Rust side should implement robust error handling using Result<T, JsValue> to catch invalid inputs before they propagate up. Finally, when considering wasm-pack build as part of your workflow, understand that it optimizes for typical web scenarios but may introduce overhead if you are targeting specialized environments like embedded systems or serverless functions where minimal footprint is critical. The documentation assumes you already know how to compile Rust code into WASM, so do not waste time on basic setup steps; instead, focus on understanding the implications of your export strategy and how JsValue behaves under different runtime conditions.
